body: You could get £86.45 a week if you care for someone at least 35 hours a week and they get certain benefits. You need to spend at least 35 hours a week caring for someone. All of the following must apply: You're 16 or over. You're not in full-time education. You're not studying for 21 hours a week or more. Your earnings are £204 or less a week after tax, National Insurance and expenses. If you live in Scotland, you need to apply for Carer Support Payment instead of Carer's Allowance.
heading: Carer's Allowance eligibility

body: Disability Living Allowance (DLA) for children may help with the extra costs of looking after a child who is under 16. The DLA rate is between £30.30 and £194.60 a week.
citation_label: GOV.UK Disability Living Allowance (DLA) for children
heading: Disability Living Allowance (DLA) for children
legal_identifier: GOV.UK Disability Living Allowance (DLA) for children

body: Care component weekly rates: lowest £30.30 for help for some of the day; middle £76.70 for frequent help or continual supervision during the day, supervision at night; highest £114.60 for help or supervision throughout both day and night. Mobility component weekly rates: lower £30.30 where the child is 5 years or over; higher £80.00 where the child is 3 years or over.
heading: DLA rates for children

body:
You can pay voluntary National Insurance to fill or avoid gaps in your record.

If you’re paying voluntary contributions for the previous 2 tax years (2023 to 2024 or 2022 to 2023), you’ll pay the original rates for those years.

For all other years you’ll pay the current rate (2024 to 2025).

£ per week 2026 to 2027 2025 to 2026 2024 to 2025 2023 to 2024 Class 3 rate £18.40 £17.75 £17.45 £17.45
citation_label: Rates and allowances: National Insurance contributions 11
heading: 4. Class 3 National Insurance (voluntary)

metadata.source_note: Official HMRC guidance page publishing National Insurance contribution rates and allowances per tax year, including the Class 3 voluntary flat weekly rate (2026 to 2027: GBP 18.40; 2025 to 2026: GBP 17.75). Annual re-ratings are made by administrative order and are not consolidated into the SSCBA 1992 s.13(1) base text on legislation.gov.uk (which still reads GBP 17.75), so this page carries the money atoms for Class 3 weekly amounts from 6 April 2026. Page last updated 6 April 2026.
metadata.title: Rates and allowances: National Insurance contributions

body:
You can view National Insurance rates and thresholds for previous tax years .

There will be some new National Insurance rates and thresholds from 6 April 2026.
citation_label: Rates and allowances: National Insurance contributions 13
heading: 5. Historical and future rates

Annual re-ratings are made by administrative order and are not consolidated into the SSCBA 1992 s.13(1) base text on legislation.gov.uk (which still reads GBP 17.75), so this page carries the money atoms for Class 3 weekly amounts from 6 April 2026. body: You'll need 10 qualifying years on your National Insurance record to get any new State Pension. The qualifying years on your National Insurance record affect how much State Pension you get.
citation_label: GOV.UK The new State Pension: eligibility
heading: The new State Pension: Eligibility
legal_identifier: GOV.UK The new State Pension: eligibility

body: The full rate of new State Pension is £241.30 a week. If your National Insurance record started after April 2016 you will need 35 qualifying years to get the full rate of new State Pension. If you were contracted out, you will usually need more than 35 qualifying years to get the full rate of new State Pension.
citation_label: GOV.UK The new State Pension: what you'll get
heading: The new State Pension: What you'll get
legal_identifier: GOV.UK The new State Pension: what you'll get

body: To be eligible you must live in England, Scotland or Wales and you've reached State Pension age.
heading: Pension Credit: Eligibility

body: Pension Credit tops up: your weekly income to £238 if you're single; your joint weekly income to £363.25 if you have a partner. The top up and extra amounts are known as 'Guarantee Credit'.
citation_label: GOV.UK Pension Credit: what you'll get
heading: Pension Credit: What you'll get
legal_identifier: GOV.UK Pension Credit: what you'll get

body: The full basic State Pension is £184.90 per week. If you're a man you usually need: 30 qualifying years if you were born between 1945 and 1951. If you're a woman you usually need: 30 qualifying years if you were born between 1950 and 1953. If you have fewer than the full number of qualifying years, your basic State Pension will be less than £184.90 per week. If you're a man born on or after 6 April 1951 or a woman born on or after 6 April 1953, you'll get the new State Pension instead.
citation_label: GOV.UK The basic State Pension: how much you get
heading: The basic State Pension: How much you get
legal_identifier: GOV.UK The basic State Pension: how much you get

body: You can ask for a refund if you have paid more than the total amount you owe. HM Revenue and Customs will tell your employer to stop taking repayments from your salary when you have repaid your loan in full, but salary deductions can take around four weeks to stop, so you may pay back more than you owe. You can ask for a refund if you made repayments but your income over the whole tax year, 6 April to 5 April the following year, was less than the annual threshold for your plan.
citation_label: GOV.UK student loan repayments: getting a refund
heading: Getting a refund

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: Getting a refund

body: How much you repay depends on your income: the amount you earn, including bonuses and overtime, before tax and other deductions. You repay a percentage of your income over the income threshold for your type of loan. The yearly income thresholds are: Plan 1, £26,900; Plan 2, £29,385; Plan 4, £33,795; Plan 5, £25,000; Postgraduate Loan, £21,000. You repay 9% of your income over the threshold if you are on Plan 1, Plan 2, Plan 4, or Plan 5. You repay 6% of your income over the threshold if you are on a Postgraduate Loan plan. If you are self-employed or complete a Self Assessment tax return for another reason, HMRC will work out how much you repay each year from your Self Assessment tax return. Your repayments will be based on your income for the whole year.
citation_label: GOV.UK student loan repayments: what you pay
heading: What you pay

metadata.title: Repaying your student loan: What you pay

body: You can get Carer Support Payment if you work. Your 'take home pay' cannot be more than £204 a week. This is about the same as £884 a month, or £10,608 a year.
citation_label: mygov.scot Carer Support Payment: if you work
heading: Carer Support Payment: If you work
legal_identifier: mygov.scot Carer Support Payment: if you work

body: You can apply for Carer Support Payment if you provide unpaid care for 35 hours or more a week and you must be aged 16 or over. You must usually live in Scotland. The person you care for must get a qualifying disability benefit. Up to £86.45 a week. It has replaced Carer's Allowance Supplement.
citation_label: mygov.scot Carer Support Payment: who can apply
heading: Carer Support Payment: Who can apply
legal_identifier: mygov.scot Carer Support Payment: who can apply

body: Scottish Child Payment is a weekly payment of £28.20 that you can get for every child you look after who's under 16 years of age. You'll get the payment every 4 weeks if your application is successful. Only one person can get Scottish Child Payment for a child.
heading: Scottish Child Payment

body: You can apply whether you're in work or not, as long as you live in Scotland, you or your partner are the main person looking after a child who's under 16 years old, and you or your partner are getting one of the qualifying benefits, such as Universal Credit. Child Benefit on its own is not an accepted benefit.
citation_label: mygov.scot Scottish Child Payment: who should apply
heading: Scottish Child Payment: Who should apply
legal_identifier: mygov.scot Scottish Child Payment: who should apply

body:
That the Parliament agrees that, for the purposes of section 11A of the Income Tax Act 2007 (which provides for Income Tax to be charged at Scottish rates on certain non-savings and non-dividend income of a Scottish taxpayer to be charged above the personal allowance), the Scottish rates and limits for the tax year 2026-27 are as follows—

(a) a starter rate of 19%, charged on income up to a limit of £3,967,

(b) the Scottish basic rate is 20%, charged on income above £3,967 and up to a limit of £16,956

(c) an intermediate rate of 21%, charged on income above £16,956 and up to a limit of £31,092,

(d) a higher rate of 42%, charged on income above £31,092 and up to a limit of £62,430,

(e) an advanced rate of 45%, charged on income above £62,430 and up to a limit of £125,140, and

(f) a top rate of 48%, charged on income above £125,140.
citation_label: Scottish Rate Resolution 2026 to 2027 - draft motion and explanatory note (draft motion) 1
heading: Shona Robison, MSP : Scottish Rate Resolution –

metadata.source_note: Official Scottish Government publication of the draft Scottish Rate Resolution motion under section 80C of the Scotland Act 1998, setting the non-savings non-dividend income tax rates and band limits for Scottish taxpayers for tax year 2026-27. Published 13 January 2026; the motion in these terms was debated and agreed by the Scottish Parliament on 19 February 2026. This page carries the verbatim rate/band table (starter, basic, intermediate, higher, advanced, top).
metadata.title: Scottish Rate Resolution 2026 to 2027 - draft motion and explanatory note (draft motion)
